metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve whether `mid-handback` is a new enum value to add to `phase` (requiring S2b amendment) or a named sub-state of `submitting`; document the answer in S5d's crash-recovery table so the test AC can unambiguously specify the kill-point.
findings.finding: `mid-handback` is used as a kill-point phase in S5d's crash-recovery decision table and in the crash-recovery test AC, but it has no corresponding value in the `phase` enum defined by S2b (`{selecting, rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, idle, paused}`). This creates an undefined state: an executor writing the crash-recovery test must either invent a new phase value or treat 'mid-handback' as a sub-state of an existing phase — with no guidance on which.
findings.location: S5d (38be-a778-43f5-4a4e) AC4 — crash-recovery decision table; crash-recovery test AC
findings.suggested_fix: If mid-handback is a sub-state of 'submitting', say so explicitly: 'kill-point: phase=submitting AND acknowledged=false (mid-handback sub-state)'. If it is a distinct phase, add it to the S2b enum and cross-reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and name the concrete heartbeat file path (e.g. `{state_volume}/heartbeat.txt`) in one authoritative location (e.g. S5d's AC or a shared constants file) and reference it explicitly in S5c's status endpoint AC and the HEALTHCHECK command.
findings.finding: The heartbeat file name/path is never contracted between S5d (which writes it) and S5c (which reads it for `heartbeat_age_s`) and S5d's HEALTHCHECK (which stat-s it). All three refer to 'a timestamp file in the state volume' or 'the heartbeat write' without specifying the concrete filename, creating an implicit shared state gap that could result in each component using a different path.
findings.location: child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (S5d) AC Heartbeat; child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 (S5c) AC Read-only status endpoint
findings.suggested_fix: In S5d's Heartbeat AC, pin the filename: 'the bot writes `{mount}/heartbeat.txt` (a UTC ISO-8601 timestamp)'; in S5c's status endpoint AC add: 'reads `heartbeat_age_s` from `{mount}/heartbeat.txt`'; in S5d's HEALTHCHECK AC add: 'stats `{mount}/heartbeat.txt`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the status endpoint's port, bind address (loopback vs. all-interfaces), nginx proxy location block (if externally reachable), and how human/admin operators authenticate to it — OR add an explicit justified no-auth rationale (e.g. 'loopback-only; only agents co-located on the same host reach it via 127.0.0.1:PORT').
findings.finding: The new HTTP status endpoint stood up by S5c has no specified port, bind address, nginx proxy path, or human/admin authentication mechanism. The plan says 'bound so agents can reach it' and 'read-only, non-secret' but never states whether it is loopback-only, behind the existing nginx TLS gateway, or internet-reachable — and never provides a named auth mechanism or an explicit justified no-auth rationale for human/admin access.
findings.location: S5c (child ticket 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03) AC1: 'Read-only status endpoint … bound so agents can reach it'; parent S5 AC: 'Observability + status endpoint'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S5c AC1: bind the status endpoint on 127.0.0.1:<PORT> (loopback-only); add a `/autolander/` location to `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` proxying to it if remote agent access is required; state explicitly 'no auth needed because the endpoint is behind the nginx TLS gateway and exposes only non-secret read-only data' as the no-auth rationale for human/admin access.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the status endpoint's explicit access class in S5c AC1 — state whether it is internal-only (and which bind address / network scope), public-with-rationale, or requires authentication, so implementers have a clear contract rather than inferring from 'non-secret'.
findings.finding: The read-only HTTP status endpoint (S5c) is network-reachable by agents but its access posture is never formally declared — the plan says 'Read-only, non-secret' but does not state whether it is public-with-rationale, internal-only, or requires any access control, nor does it name the bind address or network scope.
findings.location: S5c child ticket (9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03) — AC1 (Read-only status endpoint)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S5c AC1: 'The endpoint MUST bind to the host's loopback or internal Docker network interface only (not 0.0.0.0), is classified internal-only / unauthenticated, and is reachable only from agents co-located on the same host or private network.' If wider access is intended, state the rationale explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct S5b's title to remove 'All-Projects capability' and replace with the accurate scope: 'S5b Credentials (SSM) + Rebase ACL on rebar project refs/heads/*'.
findings.finding: S5b's ticket title reads 'S5b Credentials (SSM) + All-Projects capability + submit ACL provisioning' but the description and all ACs explicitly state the grant goes to the rebar project's own refs/meta/config — NOT All-Projects, NOT a global capability. 'All-Projects capability' in the title is a stale residual reference that directly contradicts the implementation intent.
findings.location: child ea61-01c3-daea-4824 (S5b) — ticket title vs. AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Rename S5b to: 'S5b Credentials (SSM) + Rebase access right on rebar project refs/heads/*'.

findings.finding: The read-only HTTP status endpoint introduced in S5c is a network-reachable surface (`land`'s `lander_down` reads it remotely; the AC says 'bound so agents can reach it') but its access posture is never explicitly declared — the plan says 'Read-only, non-secret' but does not state whether it is public-with-rationale, internal-network-only, or loopback-restricted. Under the rubric, an undeclared access posture on a boundary-crossing path is itself the finding, regardless of whether the data is sensitive.
findings.location: S5c AC — 'Read-only status endpoint': 'bound so agents can reach it'; S5c child ticket description
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c's AC for the status endpoint, add: 'The endpoint MUST bind to 127.0.0.1 (or the Docker-internal network only) and NOT be published on a host port reachable from outside the Gerrit box, so no auth or TLS is required; if cross-host access is needed (e.g., land running on a different host), document TLS-or-SSH-tunnel requirement.' This makes the access posture explicit and auditable.

findings.finding: The new CloudWatch alarms in monitoring_autolander.tf (NEW) are never specified to wire alarm_actions to the shared aws_sns_topic.alerts, so they could be created as silent alarms — directly contradicting the story's 'LOUD failures' and 'highly visible' reliability posture.
findings.location: S5c AC: 'Error metric (LOUD by default)' / 'Alarm in monitoring_autolander.tf' / S5 parent plan: 'fail LOUDLY'
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c's AC for the error-metric alarm and the stuck-state alarm, add: 'alarm_actions and ok_actions reference aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn (the shared alert sink declared in monitoring.tf), consistent with the repo alarm convention.'

findings.finding: The AC 'The service NEVER writes session_log' is a non-deferred, story-level requirement, but no verification mechanism is specified anywhere in the plan (no test assertion, no CI lint/grep rule, no log-output check). Without a falsifiable proof, this goal is unobservable at acceptance time.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — final bullet: 'The service NEVER writes session_log; all failure modes … are visible via metric/alarm for troubleshooting.'
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c or S5d, add a test (e.g., `test_no_session_log_handler`) that instantiates the loop service and asserts `session_log` is not among its log handlers; optionally add a `grep -r session_log infra/autolander/ && exit 1` CI step so the prohibition is enforced statically.

findings.finding: The title's headline deliverable — 'service logs, not session_log' — lacks a concrete, end-state-observable proof in the plan body. No log-routing config (compose log-driver, stdout redirect) is specified, no test asserts the absence of session_log writes, and no CI/smoke check is called out. This means the team cannot objectively verify the headline guarantee at deploy time.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — final bullet: 'The service NEVER writes session_log'
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c or S5d, add an AC line such as: 'A CI lint/grep step asserts no `session_log` import or call exists in `infra/autolander/`; the compose service definition sets `logging.driver: json-file` (or the site standard) to route container stdout/stderr to service logs.' Alternatively, add a unit test that monkeypatches `session_log` and asserts it is never called during a loop iteration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK` marker format (field names, a concrete example log line) in S5c's AC2 or a shared signal contract, and specify which code path in `loop.py` emits it so both the emitter (loop.py) and the consumer (observability.sh) have a precise contract.
findings.finding: The `AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK` structured stdout marker is named as one of the three `autolander_errors` feeders in S5c and described as emitted 'on every stack hand-back,' but its emission trigger, exact format, and who emits it are never specified in S5 or any child. S2b defines hand-back as label-removal + comment + marker-write with no mention of a structured stdout signal. An implementor cannot write the observability.sh grep/parser for this feeder without knowing the marker's structure.
findings.location: S5c (9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03) AC2 — Error metric feeders, feeder (2)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S5c AC2: 'AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK marker format: `{"marker": "AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK", "stack_id": "...", "change_ids": [...], "reason": "needs_rebase|ci_failed", "ts": "..."}` emitted to stdout immediately before label-removal in loop.py's hand-back path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in S5d's crash-recovery table whether `recovery.json`'s `acknowledged` field is the same record as S3's outcome marker or a separate field, and specify exactly which store(s) the restart reconciliation reads to determine mid-handback state.
findings.finding: The `recovery.json` schema's `acknowledged` flag is co-owned by two separate sub-plans with no co-located decision rule: S5d says the drain writes `acknowledged=false` on SIGTERM and the restart reconciliation sets `acknowledged=true` after Autosubmit-removal; S3 (darksome-starry-sable) also defines an `acknowledged` flag on its outcome markers with the same semantics. The plan never clarifies whether `recovery.json`'s `acknowledged` and S3's marker `acknowledged` are the same field written to the same store or two different records, leaving the restart-reconciliation protocol ambiguous for the mid-handback case.
findings.location: S5d (38be-a778-43f5-4a4e) AC4 — drain + crash-safe recovery; mid-handback recovery rule
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to S5d AC4: 'On restart for mid-handback: read S3's outcome marker for the stack (keyed by change_id+patchset_SHA); the `acknowledged` field there is the authoritative source. recovery.json's `acknowledged` is redundant/mirror — or remove it from recovery.json's schema if S3's marker is always the source of truth.'

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the transport protocol for the status endpoint in S5c AC1 — either confirm HTTP-only is acceptable because the endpoint is strictly loopback/internal-Docker-network-bound (and state that explicitly), or require HTTPS with a self-signed or internal CA cert if the endpoint is reachable across host boundaries.
findings.finding: The status endpoint's transport protocol is unspecified — the plan never states whether it uses HTTP or HTTPS, leaving open the possibility of plaintext transmission of operational metadata (wipChain, change IDs, heartbeat_age_s) across any network segment the endpoint is reachable from.
findings.location: S5c child ticket (9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03) — AC1 (Read-only status endpoint); Design context (heartbeat contract / status model)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the container name used in `observability.sh`'s `docker inspect` call to the compose service name defined in S5a (`autolander`), and add a test asserting the name matches.
findings.finding: S5c's `observability.sh` feeder (3) reads the container health state via `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'` but neither S5c nor S5a pins the container/service name that must be passed to `docker inspect`. S5a defines the compose service name as `autolander`, but this is not explicitly cross-referenced in S5c's feeder design. If the service is ever renamed or the inspect command uses a different name, the feeder silently stops working with no test catching it.
findings.location: child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 (S5c) — AC Error metric feeder (3); child 08e1-5505-d898-4192 (S5a) — docker-compose.yml service name
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c's feeder (3), specify the exact command: `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}' autolander` referencing S5a's service name; add a test that runs docker compose config to extract the service name and asserts it matches the name in observability.sh.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a positive rationale in S5c for why `autolander_errors` uses the journald-grep feeder in `observability.sh` (rather than direct `PutMetricData` calls in `loop.py` as used for the other two autolander metrics), and acknowledge the up-to-5-min detection lag; if the lag is unacceptable for the 'LOUD' posture, consider emitting `autolander_errors` directly from `loop.py` on the same poll cadence as the other metrics.
findings.finding: The plan assigns autolander_errors metric emission to the external observability.sh journald-grep approach (5-minute probe cadence), while autolander_time_in_phase_seconds and autolander_time_to_land_seconds are emitted directly via PutMetricData from loop.py. This asymmetry means error and handback events are only reported with up to 5-minute lag, which undercuts the plan's stated 'LOUD failures' / high-visibility posture — and no positive rationale for the split is given (e.g., 'we choose grep to avoid giving the container AWS credentials' or '5-min lag is acceptable because the stuck-state alarm provides sub-minute coverage').
findings.location: S5c AC — Error metric (LOUD by default) / observability.sh feeders (1) and (2)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a rationale note to S5c's error-metric AC, e.g.: 'We use the observability.sh grep approach (consistent with voter_errors) to avoid giving the autolander container AWS credentials; the up-to-5-min lag is acceptable because the stuck-state alarm (autolander_time_in_phase_seconds, emitted every 15s poll) provides near-real-time coverage of wedged states, and autoheal catches hangs.' Alternatively, move AUTOLANDER_ERROR/AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK emission to direct PutMetricData calls in loop.py alongside the other metrics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the heartbeat poll interval and `lander_down` age threshold configurable (e.g., env vars `AUTOLANDER_POLL_INTERVAL_S` / `AUTOLANDER_LANDER_DOWN_AGE_S`) and document the 6× coupling rule, mirroring the configurability already provided for the stuck-state threshold.
findings.finding: The heartbeat poll interval (15 s) and `lander_down` threshold (90 s) are labeled 'design-fixed' and treated as constants, but they are coupled: if the poll interval changes the 6× multiplier must change too. Neither value is specified as configurable, unlike the stuck-state threshold which is explicitly noted as configurable.
findings.location: Design context — 'Heartbeat contract (design-fixed)' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In S5a's compose file expose `AUTOLANDER_POLL_INTERVAL_S=15` and `AUTOLANDER_LANDER_DOWN_AGE_S=90`; derive `lander_down` in `land` from the latter env var or the status-endpoint's published threshold field rather than a hardcoded constant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a staged activation path for the auto-lander: e.g., an initial dry-run mode (log intended actions, skip actual submit), a per-project allowlist env var, or a canary phase limited to one low-risk project before full activation.
findings.finding: The plan deploys a service that will autonomously rebase and submit Gerrit changes with no staged rollout, dry-run mode, or per-project gate to progressively validate behavior before it acts on all eligible changes in production.
findings.location: Scope section; AC bullet S5a
findings.suggested_fix: Add `AUTOLANDER_DRY_RUN=true` and `AUTOLANDER_PROJECT_ALLOWLIST` env vars in S5a's compose spec; the first production deploy runs with dry-run enabled (or allowlist = one pilot project) and graduates to full scope after an operator sign-off window.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback runbook (at minimum a bullet list in the plan or S5d AC) covering: (1) drain-then-stop sequence, (2) state-volume retention vs. purge decision, (3) Gerrit label cleanup for any in-flight changes, and (4) how to re-enable after rollback.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a deployment rollback procedure: what operator steps remove the service, what happens to the state volume, and whether in-flight chain state (partially rebased wipChain) is left in a safe condition for manual recovery.
findings.location: AC bullet S5d — 'drain-on-SIGTERM with a concrete recovery record'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale in S5b for why AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN is added to the shared .env (e.g., 'consistent with the single .env model, reduces fetch-secrets.sh complexity, acceptable given single-host isolation') or, if least-privilege is a concern, specify a dedicated autolander env-file fetched from SSM separately.
findings.finding: The plan places AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN into the existing shared infra/compose/.env alongside ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN, MCP_HMAC_SIGNING_KEY, and other secrets, meaning the autolander container will receive all of these via env_file — credentials it has no need for. No positive rationale is given for reusing the shared .env rather than a dedicated autolander env-file.
findings.location: S5b AC — Credential: fetch-secrets.sh mapping; Design context — shared .env

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the unverified capability assertion in S5b AC3 by citing a concrete, checkable artifact (e.g. the Gerrit 3.14.1 `access-control.html` URL or a captured `GET /a/projects/rebar/access` REST response) that confirms `rebase` is a valid `[access]` token on refs/heads/* for this instance, distinct from the `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` submit-strategy key.
findings.finding: S5b asserts that the `rebase` access-right token exists in Gerrit 3.14.1's `project.config` syntax ('confirmed in this instance's `access-control.html`') and that the concrete stanza is `[access "refs/heads/*"] rebase = group Contributors` — but this is an unverified capability assertion. No `rebase =` line exists anywhere in the repo's `infra/gerrit/project.config`, no prior grant of this form appears in any gerrit config in the codebase, and the plan's only evidence is a self-referential 'confirmed' with no linked document, URL, or captured API response.
findings.location: S5b (ea61-01c3-daea-4824) AC3: 'Rebase-on-behalf access right (concrete)' — 'confirmed in this instance's access-control.html'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cross-reference in S5's Design context (or each affected child AC) to S2b's canonical `phase ∈ {selecting, rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, idle, paused}` definition so S5c's alarm filter and S5d's recovery decision table are anchored to the enum.
findings.finding: The `phase` enum value vocabulary (`{selecting, rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, idle, paused}`) is defined in S2b (a9e3-580c-409c-4eee AC1) but is never cited, cross-referenced, or co-located in S5 or any of its four child tasks. S5c's stuck-state alarm must filter 'non-`idle` phase', S5d's crash-recovery table enumerates per-phase decision rules, and the status endpoint serves `phase` — all consumers need the enum but the S5 plan body never anchors them to it.
findings.location: S5 Design context ('Status model'); S5c AC1 ('the current wipChain... phase'); S5d AC4 ('crash-recovery decision table per kill-point phase')
findings.suggested_fix: In the S5 Design context, add: 'Phase enum (canonical definition in S2b / a9e3-580c-409c-4eee AC1): `phase ∈ {selecting, rebasing, awaiting_verified, submitting, idle, paused}`.' Reference this inline in S5c AC2 ('while non-`idle` phase') and S5d's crash-recovery table.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or test in S5d asserting that all new loop.py code paths (heartbeat, sentinel, drain, crash-recovery) emit only to stdout and never write session_log.
findings.finding: S5d edits `loop.py` with new code paths (heartbeat write, sentinel check, SIGTERM drain, recovery record write, restart reconciliation) but has no AC or test asserting these paths never write `session_log`. The parent's final acceptance criterion — 'The service NEVER writes session_log' — is therefore not verified for S5d's contributions to loop.py.
findings.location: child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (S5d) — Acceptance Criteria; parent AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S5d's Tests AC: 'a test asserts that triggering heartbeat write, sentinel check, SIGTERM drain, and recovery record write produces no session_log writes (mock/assert on the session_log write path).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add S5a as a formal dependency of S5d (or state that S5d's Dockerfile.autolander edit is blocked-by S5a) to enforce the file-creation-before-edit ordering.
findings.finding: S5a and S5d both edit `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander` with no declared dependency between them — S5a creates the file (deferring HEALTHCHECK to S5d) and S5d edits it to add the HEALTHCHECK. Neither ticket lists the other as a dep (`deps: []` on both). This is an ordering violation: if S5d is worked first or merged first, it edits a file that does not yet exist.
findings.location: child 08e1-5505-d898-4192 (S5a) and child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (S5d) — Files sections
findings.suggested_fix: Add `deps: ['08e1-5505-d898-4192']` to S5d's ticket, or annotate in S5d's Files section: 'S5a must be merged first; this edit assumes the file exists.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an integration contract between S5c and S5d for their shared loop.py edits: specify thread model (e.g. HTTP server in daemon thread, SIGTERM handler signals the main loop), shared state fields (heartbeat file path, phase variable), and merge order.
findings.finding: S5c and S5d both edit `infra/autolander/loop.py` with no declared dependency or integration contract between them. S5c adds an HTTP status endpoint thread and error markers; S5d adds heartbeat writes, sentinel checks, SIGTERM handler, and recovery reconciliation. Neither ticket lists the other as a dep (`deps: []` on both). Concurrent or independent implementation risks merge conflicts, thread-safety issues (e.g. SIGTERM handler vs. HTTP server thread), or silent semantic override.
findings.location: child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 (S5c) and child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (S5d) — Files sections
findings.suggested_fix: Add S5c as a dependency of S5d (or vice versa, with the earlier child defining the loop.py skeleton/interface), and document the threading contract (SIGTERM handler + HTTP thread co-existence) in the later child's design notes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add S3 (`0525-6d71-8969-4512`) as a formal dependency of S5d, and explicitly reference the S3 marker schema (field names, write API) in S5d's AC for crash-safe recovery.
findings.finding: S5d's crash-safe recovery references writing the outcome to 'S3's marker' and reading 'S3's acknowledged=false' state during restart reconciliation, but S5d lists `deps: []` — no dependency on S3 (ticket `0525-6d71-8969-4512`). The S3 marker schema (fields, write API, acknowledged flag semantics) needed for recovery.json and restart reconciliation is owned by S3 but not formally consumed by S5d. An S5d implementer working independently may use an incompatible schema.
findings.location: child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (S5d) — AC Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery; deps: []
findings.suggested_fix: Add `deps: ['0525-6d71-8969-4512']` to S5d's ticket and add a line to the crash-safe recovery AC: 'The S3 marker schema (fields: change_id, acknowledged, phase, …) is as defined by S3; recovery.json mirrors this schema.'

findings.finding: The Gerrit `Rebase` access right (needed for `rebase_on_behalf_of_uploader=true`) is a new integration surface on this specific Gerrit 3.14.1 instance that has never been used before. The plan explicitly defers verification to implementation ('Verify at implementation the exact access-right name/form for on-behalf-of-uploader rebase against this instance's `access-control.html`'). No probe of the live instance's `access-control.html` was done, and the access-right name, the exact `project.config` stanza, and whether a separate per-change-owner permission form is required are all unconfirmed. Neither `project.config` nor `setup-project.sh` currently contain the `Rebase` right.
findings.location: S5b AC: 'Rebase-on-behalf access right (corrected mechanism): … **Verify at implementation** the exact access-right name/form for on-behalf-of-uploader rebase against this instance's `access-control.html`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `SITE_SUBDIRS` in the S5a AC (or Design context): state its format (e.g., space-separated list of directory names), where it is sourced (env var, config file), and provide a representative example value.
findings.finding: The AC bullet for S5a references `compose-up.sh SITE_SUBDIRS` without defining what SITE_SUBDIRS is — an executor cannot determine its expected value or form (a list of directory names? an env var? a file?) from this plan text.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5a bullet: '(+ `compose-up.sh SITE_SUBDIRS`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical such as: 'SITE_SUBDIRS: space-separated list of per-site subdirectories under /srv/compose, e.g. `gerrit rebar`; passed as a shell argument to compose-up.sh to scope which stacks are restarted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'a concrete recovery record' in the S5d AC with a specific observable artifact (e.g., 'a `RECOVERY.md` runbook entry documenting the crash-safe restart procedure' or 'a structured JSON recovery-state file written to the state volume on clean shutdown') so the deliverable is unambiguously verifiable.
findings.finding: The S5d AC bullet refers to 'a concrete recovery record' as a deliverable but does not define what artifact form this takes (a Markdown runbook section, a structured log entry, a state file in the volume, etc.), making it unmeasurable as stated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5d bullet: 'drain-on-SIGTERM with a concrete recovery record'
findings.suggested_fix: Example rewrite: 'drain-on-SIGTERM with a documented recovery procedure (a `RECOVERY.md` section covering the crash-safe restart steps and the state-volume invariants an operator must verify before restarting).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the three `autolander_errors` feeders (e.g., rebase failure, submit failure, unhandled exception) in the plan body or S5c AC so the criterion is self-contained and verifiable.
findings.finding: S5c AC references 'autolander_errors (three feeders + alarm)' but the plan body nowhere enumerates or describes what the three feeders are; a reader implementing S5c cannot verify the criterion without external knowledge.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5c bullet: 'autolander_errors (three feeders + alarm)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the S5c AC or Design Context: 'three feeders: (1) rebase/submit API error, (2) unhandled exception in the poll loop, (3) stuck-state alarm transition' (or whatever the intended three are).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a definition for `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` (what it measures, start/stop events, CloudWatch namespace/unit) to the Design Context or S5c AC so it is fully specified.
findings.finding: `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` is named in the S5c AC as a required deliverable but is not described anywhere in the plan body — its definition (what it measures, when it is emitted, its unit) is absent, making it an orphan criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5c bullet: 'the named autolander_time_to_land_seconds metric'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC (or expand S5d) requiring that drain/restart reconciliation reads and acts on S3's outcome marker, closing the gap between the declared S3 dependency and the verifiable acceptance criteria.
findings.finding: The Dependencies section asserts S3's outcome marker is used for 'drain/restart reconciliation', but none of the four ACs (S5a–S5d) verify that S3's outcome marker is read or acted upon during drain/restart — creating a cross-section gap between declared dependencies and the acceptance criteria.
findings.location: Dependencies section ('Uses S3's outcome marker for drain/restart reconciliation') vs. S5d AC ('drain-on-SIGTERM with a concrete recovery record')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or CLAUDE.md / design-doc section) capturing: the heartbeat timestamp-file contract and its 6× multiplier rationale, the accepted Docker-socket-write risk for autoheal, the flock single-writer guarantee, and the emergency-stop sentinel interface — so future maintainers can change thresholds or swap patterns safely.
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel architectural decisions (heartbeat timestamp-file contract, autoheal Docker-socket-write pattern, single-writer flock guard, HTTP status endpoint wiring `heartbeat_age_s` to `lander_down`, emergency-stop sentinel, drain-on-SIGTERM recovery record) but mentions no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture them for future maintainers.
findings.location: Design context section; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0NNN-autolander-deploy-contracts.md` covering: (1) heartbeat file path + freshness thresholds + why 6×; (2) autoheal socket-write privilege + mitigations; (3) flock guard rationale; (4) status-endpoint field contract (`heartbeat_age_s`) that `land` depends on. Reference the ADR from CLAUDE.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence justification (in S5a or the Design context) explaining that `restart: unless-stopped` only restarts on container EXIT — not on HEALTHCHECK `unhealthy` — and that autoheal is required precisely to restart a live-but-hung (wedged) bot that never exits, making the socket-write cost load-bearing.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `willfarrell/autoheal` as a novel sidecar (with Docker socket-write access) without explicitly justifying it over the established `restart: unless-stopped` pattern already used by every service in the stack. The critical distinction — that compose's `restart: unless-stopped` only triggers on container EXIT, not on HEALTHCHECK `unhealthy` state, whereas autoheal monitors health-state — is the actual justification for the novelty but is never stated. Without this rationale the plan reads as repackaging a sidecar around a problem the existing pattern could handle, and the socket-write privilege cost appears unjustified.
findings.location: Design context / Autoheal socket bullet; S5a AC4 (willfarrell/autoheal sidecar wired)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design context 'Autoheal socket' bullet, add: 'Note: `restart: unless-stopped` (the existing compose pattern) only triggers on container exit — it does NOT restart a live-but-hung container in `unhealthy` health state. Autoheal fills this gap by watching health state and issuing `docker restart`; this is the architectural reason the socket-write privilege is required and not avoidable with the existing restart policy.'

findings.finding: The story-level AC bullet for S5c states that failure modes must be 'visible via metric/alarm', and names a 'stuck-state time-in-phase alarm', but neither the story nor the Design Context specifies the time-in-phase threshold that triggers the alarm — leaving the executor to choose an arbitrary value.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5c bullet; Design Context — 'stuck-state alarm' reference
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Design Context: "Stuck-state threshold: a change that has not advanced phase within 20 minutes triggers the `autolander_stuck_phase` alarm (configurable via env var `STUCK_PHASE_TIMEOUT_S`, default 1200)."

findings.finding: The stuck-state alarm in S5c fires 'when a `wipChain` sits in any non-`idle` phase past a threshold' but the threshold value is never stated. Unlike the heartbeat contract (which explicitly states ≤15 s freshness and >90 s lander_down), the time-in-phase threshold has no specified default, no reference to a related timeout (e.g., the S2 30-min Verified-await bound), and no unit-check anchor.
findings.location: S5c AC: 'Stuck-state alarm (high visibility)'

findings.finding: S5a specifies the Docker socket is mounted 'read-only' for the autoheal sidecar, but autoheal issues `docker restart` (a daemon write operation); a :ro socket mount makes the restart action fail silently, negating the core autoheal reliability mechanism.
findings.location: S5a AC: 'the autolander service carries the autoheal=true label so the sidecar manages it'; S5a description: 'the Docker socket mounted read-only'
findings.suggested_fix: In docker-compose.yml, change the autoheal socket volume entry from `/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro` to `/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` (no `:ro`). If blast-radius is a concern, note that autoheal only calls restart on containers it labels — it does not have broad daemon control.

findings.finding: The plan specifies the Docker socket for willfarrell/autoheal is mounted `:ro` (read-only), but autoheal's core function — restarting an unhealthy container — requires issuing `POST /containers/{id}/restart` to the Docker daemon, which is a write operation. A read-only socket mount will silently prevent autoheal from restarting the autolander container, defeating the stated reliability posture.
findings.location: S5a (08e1-5505-d898-4192) AC: 'Autoheal sidecar wired' — 'Docker socket mounted read-only'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the socket mount in docker-compose.yml from `- /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro` to `- /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` (no `:ro`), document the write-access rationale in a compose comment, and note the security trade-off (host socket access) in the design context.

findings.finding: The plan mounts the Docker socket for the `willfarrell/autoheal` sidecar as `:ro` (read-only), but autoheal must issue `POST /containers/{id}/restart` against the Docker daemon API — a write operation. A read-only Unix socket mount silently prevents all write syscalls, so the sidecar starts but never restarts an unhealthy container. The restart behaviour is then un-exercised by S5d's hang test (which asserts autoheal works), making the reliability guarantee non-functional at runtime.
findings.location: S5a AC: 'Autoheal sidecar wired: the `willfarrell/autoheal` service is present in `docker-compose.yml` with the Docker socket mounted **read-only**'
findings.suggested_fix: In docker-compose.yml, change `- /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro` to `- /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` (or `:rw`). The `:ro` annotation was likely added to limit socket exposure, but autoheal's operative function — restarting containers — requires write access to the Docker daemon API.

findings.finding: S5a mandates the willfarrell/autoheal Docker socket be mounted read-only (`:ro`), but autoheal requires write access to the Docker socket to issue container restart commands — mounting it `:ro` will silently break autoheal's ability to restart the autolander container.
findings.location: child 08e1-5505-d898-4192, AC 'Autoheal sidecar wired'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `:ro` qualifier from the Docker socket mount for the autoheal sidecar service in docker-compose.yml. The willfarrell/autoheal image requires write access (`/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock`) to call the Docker API restart endpoint.

findings.finding: S5d specifies the drain is 'time-bounded (SIGTERM deadline ~ the CI-wait timeout)', but docker compose uses a default stop_grace_period of 10 seconds before sending SIGKILL; if a CI-wait is in progress at shutdown, the bot is SIGKILLed before the drain completes and recovery.json is written, defeating the crash-safe recovery guarantee.
findings.location: S5d AC: 'Drain-on-shutdown + crash-safe recovery'; S5d description: 'the drain is time-bounded (SIGTERM deadline ~ the CI-wait timeout)'
findings.suggested_fix: In docker-compose.yml under the `autolander` service, add `stop_grace_period: <duration>` where duration ≥ the CI-wait timeout (e.g., `stop_grace_period: 600s` if CI is bounded at ~10 min). The drain itself should still enforce its own deadline and exit voluntarily before the grace period expires.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
